title: Ulcerative Colitis
oneLiner: Chronic idiopathic inflammation of colorectal mucosa; presents with bloody diarrhoea, urgency, tenesmus; requires early recognition to prevent toxic megacolon.

sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Bloody diarrhoea (often mixed with mucus)
  - Urgency & tenesmus (constant feeling of needing to pass stool)
  - Abdominal pain (crampy, left-sided or diffuse)
  - Constitutional symptoms (fever, weight loss, fatigue in active disease)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Left iliac fossa tenderness on palpation
  - Abdominal distension (if severe or megacolon)
  - Perianal inspection: normal (no fistulas/abscesses – think Crohn’s)
- **Red flags that change urgency:**
  - High fever (>38.5°C), severe tachycardia (HR >100 bpm)
  - Profuse diarrhoea (>6–8 bloody stools/day)
  - Abdominal distension, reduced or absent bowel sounds
  - Hypovolaemic shock
  - Signs of peritonism (guarding, rigidity)
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Most likely:** Infectious colitis (e.g. *C. difficile*, *Salmonella*, *Campylobacter*) – stool culture positive, recent antibiotics/hospitalisation
- **Crohn’s disease** – skip lesions, fistulas, perianal disease, non-bloody diarrhoea
- **IBS** – no blood, no nocturnal symptoms, defecation related to stress
- **Diverticulitis** – localised left-sided pain, fever, no rectal bleeding (usually)
- **Ischaemic colitis** – acute onset, older age, pain out of proportion, risk factors
- **Colorectal carcinoma** – change in bowel habit, weight loss, family history, PR bleeding alone
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**
  - Stool culture + sensitivity + *C. difficile* toxin (always before starting steroids)
  - Urine pregnancy test (if female of childbearing age)
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC (anaemia, thrombocytosis – active disease marker)
  - CRP, ESR (elevated in active disease)
  - U&Es, LFTs (dehydration, hypoalbuminaemia – severity)
  - Ferritin, B12, folate (chronic disease)
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** Abdominal X-ray (erect +/- supine) – assess for colonic dilatation (>5.5 cm = toxic megacolon), thumbprinting, loss of haustra
  - **Second-line:** CT abdomen (if suspect perforation, abscess, or toxic megacolon with clinical deterioration)
- **Special tests:**
  - Flexible sigmoidoscopy/colonoscopy (biopsy confirm – but avoid in acute severe disease due to perforation risk)
  - Faecal calprotectin (elevated – differentiates IBD from IBS)
  - CMV / EBV PCR (if refractory disease – immunocompromised)
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**
  - **Truelove & Witts severity index:**
    - Mild: <4 stools/day, no blood, no fever, normal HR/ESR
    - Moderate: 4–6 stools/day, mild blood, no systemic signs
    - Severe: >6 bloody stools/day, HR >90, Hb <10.5, ESR >30, fever >37.8°C
    - **Fulminant:** >10 stools/day, continuous bleeding, toxic megacolon, shock
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Fluid resuscitation (IV crystalloid – 1–2 L bolus if dehydrated)
  - Correct electrolytes (potassium, magnesium)
  - NBM if suspected toxic megacolon
  - Nasogastric tube if vomiting/distension
  - IV broad-spectrum antibiotics *if* febrile or septic (e.g. piperacillin-tazobactam)
  - Call gastroenterology / surgical team if severe features
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - **Mild-moderate:** Oral 5-ASA (mesalazine or balsalazide) + topical (suppository/enema)
  - **Moderate-severe:** IV corticosteroids (hydrocortisone or methylprednisolone) + ensure infection excluded
  - Monitor stool chart, family sign, daily CRP/albumin
  - If no response in 3–5 days: escalate to rescue therapy (infliximab or cyclosporine) – requires senior/GI input
  - Thromboprophylaxis (LMWH) – high thromboembolic risk
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Maintenance therapy: oral 5-ASA (mesalazine)
  - Steroid-sparing: azathioprine, 6-mercaptopurine (or biologic)
  - Biologics: anti-TNF (infliximab, adalimumab); vedolizumab; tofacitinib (JAK inhibitor)
  - Surgical referral: total colectomy if medically refractory, dysplasia, or acute complications
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents — mild-moderate:**
  - Mesalazine (oral) 2–4 g daily (divided dose for maintenance 1.2–2.4 g daily)
  - Mesalazine (suppository) 500 mg–1 g nocte for proctitis
  - Mesalazine (enema) 1–4 g nocte for left-sided disease
- **First-line agents — moderate-severe:**
  - Hydrocortisone IV 100 mg q6h (or methylprednisolone 40–60 mg IV daily)
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Budesonide (oral) 9 mg daily for mild-moderate disease (lower systemic side effects)
  - Infliximab IV 5 mg/kg at 0, 2, 6 weeks (induction) then q8 weeks
  - Cyclosporine IV 2–4 mg/kg/day (rescue for acute severe UC)
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - 5-ASA: caution in renal impairment (monitor Cr)
  - Corticosteroids: avoid in active infection, prolonged use → adrenal suppression, osteoporosis
  - Thiopurines: TPMT testing before starting; risk of myelotoxicity
  - Infliximab: screen for TB, hepatitis B, contraindicated in active TB/sepsis
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Toxic megacolon (colonic diameter >5.5 cm, loss of haustra)
  - Colonic perforation (emergency surgery)
  - Massive haemorrhage (rare)
  - Thromboembolism (DVT/PE)
- **Chronic:**
  - Colorectal cancer risk (after 8–10 years, surveillance colonoscopy q1–3 years)
  - Anaemia (iron deficiency)
  - Osteoporosis (steroid-related)
  - Hepatic (primary sclerosing cholangitis)
sections.Prognosis: - 1-2 lines on mortality / recovery / chronicity: Relapsing-remitting course; most patients are well-controlled on 5-ASA – acute severe colitis carries 1–2% mortality (higher with megacolon). Total colectomy is curative but permanent stoma or pouch.
sections.Pearls & Pitfalls:
- **Pearls:**
  - Always send stool cultures *before* starting steroids – missing *C. difficile* is a common trap.
  - Anaemia + thrombocytosis strongly suggests active disease.
  - In acute severe UC, getting a "3-day rule" (no response to IV steroids in 72h) triggers escalation.
  - Colonic distension >5.5 cm on AXR is an emergency – call surgical and GI immediately.
  - DVT prophylaxis essential – UC patients have 3-fold increased thromboembolic risk.
- **Common mistakes juniors make:**
  - Forgetting stool cultures and giving steroids blindly.
  - Not checking abdominal X-ray in severe diarrhoea – miss megacolon.
  - Using loperamide/antidiarrhoeals in active flare – risk of toxic megacolon.
  - Delaying escalation to infliximab/cyclosporine when steroids fail.
  - Not arranging colonoscopic surveillance for dysplasia after 8–10 years.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **When to call senior / ICU / crash team:**
  - NEWS2 ≥5 and/or lactat >2 mmol/L
  - GCS drop (<13)
  - Suspected toxic megacolon (colonic diameter >5.5 cm)
  - Signs of peritonism (guarding, rebound)
  - Haemodynamic instability (systolic BP <90 mmHg, HR >120)
  - Failure to improve after 3 days IV steroids (call GI registrar/consultant)
  - Severe pain requiring frequent analgesia or unable to lie flat

rawMarkdown:
## Ulcerative Colitis
**One-liner:** Chronic idiopathic inflammation of colorectal mucosa; presents with bloody diarrhoea, urgency, tenesmus; requires early recognition to prevent toxic megacolon.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Bloody diarrhoea (often mixed with mucus)
  - Urgency & tenesmus (constant feeling of needing to pass stool)
  - Abdominal pain (crampy, left-sided or diffuse)
  - Constitutional symptoms (fever, weight loss, fatigue in active disease)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Left iliac fossa tenderness on palpation
  - Abdominal distension (if severe or megacolon)
  - Perianal inspection: normal (no fistulas/abscesses – think Crohn’s)
- **Red flags that change urgency:**
  - High fever (>38.5°C), severe tachycardia (HR >100 bpm)
  - Profuse diarrhoea (>6–8 bloody stools/day)
  - Abdominal distension, reduced or absent bowel sounds
  - Hypovolaemic shock
  - Signs of peritonism (guarding, rigidity)

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Most likely:** Infectious colitis (e.g. *C. difficile*, *Salmonella*, *Campylobacter*) – stool culture positive, recent antibiotics/hospitalisation
- **Crohn’s disease** – skip lesions, fistulas, perianal disease, non-bloody diarrhoea
- **IBS** – no blood, no nocturnal symptoms, defecation related to stress
- **Diverticulitis** – localised left-sided pain, fever, no rectal bleeding (usually)
- **Ischaemic colitis** – acute onset, older age, pain out of proportion, risk factors
- **Colorectal carcinoma** – change in bowel habit, weight loss, family history, PR bleeding alone

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**
  - Stool culture + sensitivity + *C. difficile* toxin (always before starting steroids)
  - Urine pregnancy test (if female of childbearing age)
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC (anaemia, thrombocytosis – active disease marker)
  - CRP, ESR (elevated in active disease)
  - U&Es, LFTs (dehydration, hypoalbuminaemia – severity)
  - Ferritin, B12, folate (chronic disease)
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** Abdominal X-ray (erect +/- supine) – assess for colonic dilatation (>5.5 cm = toxic megacolon), thumbprinting, loss of haustra
  - **Second-line:** CT abdomen (if suspect perforation, abscess, or toxic megacolon with clinical deterioration)
- **Special tests:**
  - Flexible sigmoidoscopy/colonoscopy (biopsy confirm – but avoid in acute severe disease due to perforation risk)
  - Faecal calprotectin (elevated – differentiates IBD from IBS)
  - CMV / EBV PCR (if refractory disease – immunocompromised)
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**
  - **Truelove & Witts severity index:**
    - Mild: <4 stools/day, no blood, no fever, normal HR/ESR
    - Moderate: 4–6 stools/day, mild blood, no systemic signs
    - Severe: >6 bloody stools/day, HR >90, Hb <10.5, ESR >30, fever >37.8°C
    - **Fulminant:** >10 stools/day, continuous bleeding, toxic megacolon, shock

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Fluid resuscitation (IV crystalloid – 1–2 L bolus if dehydrated)
  - Correct electrolytes (potassium, magnesium)
  - NBM if suspected toxic megacolon
  - Nasogastric tube if vomiting/distension
  - IV broad-spectrum antibiotics *if* febrile or septic (e.g. piperacillin-tazobactam)
  - Call gastroenterology / surgical team if severe features
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - **Mild-moderate:** Oral 5-ASA (mesalazine or balsalazide) + topical (suppository/enema)
  - **Moderate-severe:** IV corticosteroids (hydrocortisone or methylprednisolone) + ensure infection excluded
  - Monitor stool chart, family sign, daily CRP/albumin
  - If no response in 3–5 days: escalate to rescue therapy (infliximab or cyclosporine) – requires senior/GI input
  - Thromboprophylaxis (LMWH) – high thromboembolic risk
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Maintenance therapy: oral 5-ASA (mesalazine)
  - Steroid-sparing: azathioprine, 6-mercaptopurine (or biologic)
  - Biologics: anti-TNF (infliximab, adalimumab); vedolizumab; tofacitinib (JAK inhibitor)
  - Surgical referral: total colectomy if medically refractory, dysplasia, or acute complications

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents — mild-moderate:**
  - Mesalazine (oral) 2–4 g daily (divided dose for maintenance 1.2–2.4 g daily)
  - Mesalazine (suppository) 500 mg–1 g nocte for proctitis
  - Mesalazine (enema) 1–4 g nocte for left-sided disease
- **First-line agents — moderate-severe:**
  - Hydrocortisone IV 100 mg q6h (or methylprednisolone 40–60 mg IV daily)
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Budesonide (oral) 9 mg daily for mild-moderate disease (lower systemic side effects)
  - Infliximab IV 5 mg/kg at 0, 2, 6 weeks (induction) then q8 weeks
  - Cyclosporine IV 2–4 mg/kg/day (rescue for acute severe UC)
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - 5-ASA: caution in renal impairment (monitor Cr)
  - Corticosteroids: avoid in active infection, prolonged use → adrenal suppression, osteoporosis
  - Thiopurines: TPMT testing before starting; risk of myelotoxicity
  - Infliximab: screen for TB, hepatitis B, contraindicated in active TB/sepsis

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Toxic megacolon (colonic diameter >5.5 cm, loss of haustra)
  - Colonic perforation (emergency surgery)
  - Massive haemorrhage (rare)
  - Thromboembolism (DVT/PE)
- **Chronic:**
  - Colorectal cancer risk (after 8–10 years, surveillance colonoscopy q1–3 years)
  - Anaemia (iron deficiency)
  - Osteoporosis (steroid-related)
  - Hepatic (primary sclerosing cholangitis)

### Prognosis
- 1-2 lines on mortality / recovery / chronicity: Relapsing-remitting course; most patients are well-controlled on 5-ASA – acute severe colitis carries 1–2% mortality (higher with megacolon). Total colectomy is curative but permanent stoma or pouch.

### Pearls & Pitfalls
- **Pearls:**
  - Always send stool cultures *before* starting steroids – missing *C. difficile* is a common trap.
  - Anaemia + thrombocytosis strongly suggests active disease.
  - In acute severe UC, getting a "3-day rule" (no response to IV steroids in 72h) triggers escalation.
  - Colonic distension >5.5 cm on AXR is an emergency – call surgical and GI immediately.
  - DVT prophylaxis essential – UC patients have 3-fold increased thromboembolic risk.
- **Common mistakes juniors make:**
  - Forgetting stool cultures and giving steroids blindly.
  - Not checking abdominal X-ray in severe diarrhoea – miss megacolon.
  - Using loperamide/antidiarrhoeals in active flare – risk of toxic megacolon.
  - Delaying escalation to infliximab/cyclosporine when steroids fail.
  - Not arranging colonoscopic surveillance for dysplasia after 8–10 years.

### Escalation Criteria
- **When to call senior / ICU / crash team:**
  - NEWS2 ≥5 and/or lactat >2 mmol/L
  - GCS drop (<13)
  - Suspected toxic megacolon (colonic diameter >5.5 cm)
  - Signs of peritonism (guarding, rebound)
  - Haemodynamic instability (systolic BP <90 mmHg, HR >120)
  - Failure to improve after 3 days IV steroids (call GI registrar/consultant)
  - Severe pain requiring frequent analgesia or unable to lie flat
